figures.gloss: A frail nineteen-year-old of Hòa Hảo village who emerged from chronic illness in 1939 healing the sick and preaching in plain verse, founding a Buddhism for peasants. He entered nationalist politics, founded the Dân Xã party in 1946, and was killed by the Việt Minh in April 1947; his body was never recovered.
figures.facet: Followers received him as the Buddha Master of Western Peace returned, and because he has no grave many still await his return — a founder whose absence is doctrinally load-bearing.
figures.provenance: Sấm Giảng Thi Văn Giáo Lý (Sổ's collected oracles and teachings); Hue-Tam Ho Tai, 'Millenarianism and Peasant Politics in Vietnam' (1983); Pascal Bourdeaux's studies of Hòa Hảo.

figures.gloss: The Mekong millenarian lineage founded by Đoàn Minh Huyên, the 'Buddha Master of Western Peace' (Phật Thầy Tây An), who preached and healed through the 1849 cholera epidemic: the world is in the low age (Hạ Nguơn), and salvation lies in plain living, amulets bearing the four-character formula, and the Seven Mountains of the frontier.
figures.facet: It is frontier religion in the exact sense — apocalyptic Buddhism fitted to peasants clearing new delta land, which is why its heirs, Hòa Hảo among them, kept erupting into politics.
figures.provenance: Hue-Tam Ho Tai, 'Millenarianism and Peasant Politics in Vietnam' (1983); temple traditions of Tây An and the Thất Sơn (Seven Mountains) region.

figures.gloss: Huỳnh Phú Sổ's sermons and prophecies, composed from 1939 onward largely in vernacular six-eight verse that unlettered farmers could memorize and chant; together with his prose teachings they form the movement's entire canon.
figures.facet: Scripture engineered for the unlettered: doctrine travels as memorized rhyme through village networks, bypassing monastery and clergy entirely.
figures.provenance: Sấm Giảng Thi Văn Giáo Lý; Hue-Tam Ho Tai (1983).

figures.gloss: Hòa Hảo worship stripped to its floor: a plain brown cloth (trần dà) in place of statues on the home altar, offerings of only fresh water, flowers, and incense, and an open-air sky altar (bàn thông thiên) in the yard facing heaven. No pagodas, no monks — the household is the temple.
figures.facet: Its motto 'Học Phật, Tu Nhân' — study the Buddha, cultivate humanity — relocates merit from monastic donation to peasant ethics, an austerity that doubled as economic protest against ritual expense.
figures.provenance: Huỳnh Phú Sổ, 'Cách Tu Hiền' (guidance on lay practice, 1945); Hue-Tam Ho Tai (1983).

figures.gloss: The moral spine of the teaching, inherited from Bửu Sơn Kỳ Hương: every believer owes four unpayable debts — to ancestors and parents, to country, to the Three Jewels of Buddhism, and to compatriots and humankind.
figures.facet: The debt to country sits inside the religious law itself, which is why Hòa Hảo piety flowed so directly into anti-colonial arms — patriotism here is not adjacent to religion but one of its four pillars.
figures.provenance: Sấm Giảng Thi Văn Giáo Lý; Hue-Tam Ho Tai (1983).

figures.gloss: The millenarian horizon: at the close of the low age, Maitreya Buddha will convene the Dragon Flower Assembly, judging humanity and opening an age of peace — the virtuous gathered in, the wicked swept away.
figures.facet: It compresses Buddhist kalpa-cosmology into an imminent local event, with the Seven Mountains as its expected stage — keeping ordinary farm labor charged with end-time significance.
figures.provenance: Sấm Giảng; Hue-Tam Ho Tai (1983); the canonical Maitreya cycle of Buddhist eschatology.

edges.note: GOLD SEAM: A documented laicization of the Mahayana vehicle — merit and awakening pursued by householders in the rice field, with monkhood and the temple economy stripped away.
GOLD SEAM: The three-age scheme and the coming assembly descend directly from Buddhist kalpa-cosmology and the canonical Maitreya prophecy, compressed to an imminent horizon.
GOLD SEAM: Both traditions await Maitreya's Dragon Flower Assembly by that very name — parallel heirs of the same East Asian Maitreyan current.
GOLD SEAM: Two Mekong-era announcements that the final age has arrived — Caodaism answers with a universal amnesty, Hòa Hảo with a judgment assembly.

GOLD SEAM: Mirror answers to representing the absolute in colonial Cochinchina — Caodaism builds a cathedral around a single divine Eye; Hòa Hảo hangs a plain brown cloth and calls the household a temple.
Intra-tradition: followers received Sổ as the Buddha Master of Western Peace returned — Hòa Hảo understands itself as this lineage's completion.
Intra-tradition: the founder's verse oracles are the movement's entire canon.

dossier.essence: A lay Buddhist millenarian religion of Vietnam's western Mekong Delta, founded in 1939 when Huỳnh Phú Sổ, a sickly nineteen-year-old of Hòa Hảo village (An Giang), emerged from illness healing and preaching a stripped-down Buddhism in peasant verse. It understands itself as the completion of the Bửu Sơn Kỳ Hương lineage begun by Đoàn Minh Huyên amid the 1849 cholera epidemic, and its founder was received as that lineage's Buddha Master returned. Sổ was killed by the Việt Minh in April 1947; the movement he left — armed and semi-autonomous in the early 1950s, broken by Diệm in 1955–56, state-recognized in 1999 — counts on the order of one to two million adherents, still concentrated in the Delta.
dossier.cosmos: The worldview is Buddhist kalpa-cosmology compressed to an imminent horizon: the world is deep in the low age (Hạ Nguơn), whose close will bring Maitreya's Dragon Flower Assembly (Hội Long Hoa) — the virtuous gathered into an age of peace, the wicked swept away — with the Seven Mountains of the frontier as its expected stage. Karma and rebirth frame the moral life, and merit is earned in the field and household rather than the monastery. Because the founder's body was never recovered, many followers await his return, an absence that is doctrinally load-bearing.
dossier.practice: Worship is domestic and austere: a plain brown cloth (trần dà) in place of images on the home altar, an open-air sky altar facing heaven, offerings of only fresh water, flowers, and incense; no pagodas, no monkhood, cheap simple rites for weddings and funerals. The ethical core is the Tứ Ân — four unpayable debts, to ancestors and parents, to country, to the Three Jewels, and to compatriots and humankind — under the motto 'Học Phật, Tu Nhân' (study the Buddha, cultivate humanity); the debt to country carried the faith directly into anti-colonial politics and arms in the 1940s, and today lay administrative committees run it under state oversight.
dossier.canon: The Sấm Giảng Thi Văn Giáo Lý — Huỳnh Phú Sổ's oracles, sermons, and prose teachings from 1939 onward, composed largely in vernacular six-eight verse for memorization — is the movement's entire canon.
dossier.reflects: Millenarian urgency domesticated into lay austerity — end-time Buddhism rebuilt as peasant ethics, with the temple economy abolished and the household made the sanctuary.
sources: Hue-Tam Ho Tai, 'Millenarianism and Peasant Politics in Vietnam' (1983)
Nguyen Long Thanh Nam, 'Hoa Hao Buddhism in the Course of Vietnam's History' (2003)
Philip Taylor (ed.), 'Modernity and Re-enchantment: Religion in Post-revolutionary Vietnam' (2007)

links.cite: Hue-Tam Ho Tai, 'Millenarianism and Peasant Politics in Vietnam' (1983)
